expectations.text: (e0-worktree-command) Response includes the `git worktree add` command
expectations.evidence: The response contains `git worktree add` multiple times, including in the step-by-step setup (e.g., `git worktree add ../myrepo-wt/feat/auth -b feat/auth`) and in the summary table.

expectations.text: (e0-two-branches) Response creates or names two distinct branches (one for auth, one for bug/fix)
expectations.evidence: The response explicitly creates two branches: `feat/auth` for the auth feature session and `fix/api-bug` for the API bug fix session.

expectations.text: (e0-agent-scoping) Response explains how to scope each agent to its own worktree directory
expectations.evidence: The response provides explicit session briefs with absolute working directory paths for each agent (e.g., `Working directory: /home/user/projects/myrepo-wt/feat/auth`) and states: 'The session brief must include the absolute working directory path so the agent never accidentally operates in the wrong place.'

expectations.text: (e0-isolation-principle) Response conveys the one-agent-one-worktree-one-branch rule or equivalent isolation advice
expectations.evidence: The response has an explicit 'Cardinal Rule' section stating: 'One agent, one worktree, one branch. Two agents sharing a worktree will corrupt each other's index. Two agents on the same branch will produce conflicting history. Keep them completely separate.'

eval_feedback: The response is comprehensive and directly addresses all four assertions. It includes the `git worktree add` command with concrete examples tailored to the user's exact use case (auth feature + API bug fix), names two distinct branches (`feat/auth` and `fix/api-bug`), clearly scopes each agent to its own directory via explicit session briefs with absolute paths, and leads with a Cardinal Rule that articulates the one-agent-one-worktree-one-branch isolation principle. All four assertions pass with strong supporting evidence.
